questions.question: Explain the purpose of the chdir system call and its return values.
keyPoints: chdir changes the current working directory of a process
Returns 0 on success
Returns -1 on failure with errno set
Used for relative path resolution

questions.question: What is the Current Working Directory (CWD)? Explain how it is inherited by child processes.
keyPoints: CWD is the default directory for relative path resolution
Every process maintains its own CWD
Child process inherits CWD from parent at fork() time
Changes in child do not affect parent and vice versa

questions.question: List and explain any three error codes returned by chdir system call.
keyPoints: ENOENT: Path does not exist
EACCES: Permission denied
ENOTDIR: Path component is not a directory
ERANGE: Buffer too small (for getcwd)

questions.question: Differentiate between chdir and fchdir system calls.
keyPoints: chdir takes pathname as argument
fchdir takes open file descriptor to directory
fchdir can be more efficient in loops
Both change the CWD of the process

questions.question: What permissions are required to successfully execute chdir on a directory?
keyPoints: Execute (search) permission needed on each path component
Write permission is NOT required
Read permission is NOT required
Permission checked for all directories in the path

questions.question: Explain the relationship between getcwd and chdir system calls.
keyPoints: getcwd returns the current working directory
chdir changes the current working directory
getcwd provides path as string, chdir takes path as input
Both are complementary operations

questions.question: What happens to open file descriptors when chdir is called?
keyPoints: Open file descriptors remain valid
File descriptors are independent of CWD
They continue to point to same files/directories
No file descriptors are automatically closed

questions.question: Write a C program that demonstrates the use of chdir and getcwd system calls. Include proper error handling.
keyPoints: Include <unistd.h> and <stdio.h>
Use chdir() with path argument
Use getcwd() to retrieve current directory
Check return values for errors
Use perror() or errno for error messages
Handle cases like directory doesn't exist, permission denied

questions.question: Explain the concept of Current Working Directory in Unix/Linux. How does it affect path resolution? What are the implications of calling chdir in a parent process versus a child process?
keyPoints: CWD is the base for relative path resolution
Relative paths are appended to CWD
Each process has independent CWD
Parent process CWD unaffected by child's chdir
Child inherits parent's CWD at fork time
Changes via chdir are process-local only

questions.question: Discuss the error handling mechanisms in chdir system call. What are the different error conditions and how should a programmer handle them?
keyPoints: Returns -1 on failure, 0 on success
errno set to specific error code
ENOENT for non-existent path
EACCES for permission issues
ENOTDIR when path component is not directory
Programmer should check return value and use perror()
Good practice to handle each error case appropriately

questions.question: Explain the significance of chdir in daemon process programming. Why do daemons typically call chdir("/")? What problems could occur if they don't?
keyPoints: Daemons run in background detached from terminal
Change to root directory prevents holding mount points
Without chdir, daemon keeps CWD preventing unmount
Cannot unmount filesystem if any process has CWD there
Common practice: chdir("/") and chroot() for isolation
Also close or redirect standard file descriptors
